My personal opinion about this toy, is that it would not be safe for a child to play with. Let's start with the bells. Only one peg is keeping them attached to the lion figure. The only portion of the toy that I would think would be somewhat safe, is the elephant. It is like a baby rattle and unless the plastic holding the beads break, it should be fine. This hedgehog took me almost a week to fully assemble. It’s made for someone with a lot of time and patience. There are so many little pieces that the whole process was very tedious. One flaw is that the battery compartment is one of the very first steps... and in order to change the batteries you need to take the whole hedgehog apart and then reassemble all over again which can be a huge pain! Once completed the hedgehog is very cool as he can walk or curl up in a ball in response to your clap commands. For the time, patience, and effort put into this I would not recommend this product as even though he’s cute it’s not worth all the effort and time. Not to mention some pieces fall out as he moves around and you have to keep on fixing, which is a pain.

I would not have been pleased had I purchased this set. The packaging is misleading in my opinion. For example, it shows a variety of colors included in this kit; but in reality we only get five colors of chalk sticks. With the exception of one red and one green "scented" stick. There is barely any scent in these two sticks; and none of the other chalk are scented from what I can tell. The chalk holders don't work well, as the chalk sticks easily fall out of the holders. The spray chalk was a temporary hit with my kids; but that only lasted about 20 minutes. Plus the instructions show measuring the powder out with a cup that isn't included. And it was a bit messy getting the powder in the spray bottle. Eventually my kids started filling the water bottle with plain water and had more fun spraying things (like each other) with it instead of using the spray chalk. The stencils are flimsy and didn't work for us. Overall, I simply can not recommend this set.

This is not made of cotton - even the homemade label (which means it is clearly not a registered product of the US) says "All new material".  And even if they were using tricky language above to say just the stuffing is cotton and not mention the covering, know that the stuffing is not cotton either.  Even those companies that try very hard to control their supply chain like Target and Walmart often have lead, arsenic, and other toxic materials snuck into their products in the unregulated countries they are made in.  SO, it is more likely that this contains something concerning when you consider the company goes out of their way to lie/make a fraudulent claim by stating here that it is cotton.  It is extra concerning and frankly scary that this is a product for children for whom metal and synthetic toxins can seriously damage brain and nerve development.  They say on the label that it passed the CPSC, CE tests but there is no tracking label - no number or logo of a certified lab, it is just an assertion.  Scary that Amazon would let someone put this in their store also.

My 6 year old niece has been struggling with her sight words. She's been doing online school since March. As a former teacher, I try to help her as much as I can. I thought an educational game would be fun. We had to punch out the cards which my niece enjoyed helping with. It was just the two of us, so once the game was ready to go, I would call out the words and then we both would search for it.  She had a lot of fun playing the game. We've only played with the lower levels of words so far, so it was more of review for her. But as the difficulty of words increases, it will be more effective as a learning tool. The game pieces are not very sturdy. If I was using this in a classroom, I would probably laminate them before letting the students use it. There is a bag to store the pieces in, although we did put the flies in a Ziploc baggie before putting it into the provided drawstring bag. Overall, I think it's a fun approach to sight words!

Over 40 songs, sounds and phrases it says on here..Although I have not notice there are that many!!  There are so many different toys out there, that just about do the samething, but this one is in a class all its own..Just being the Unicorn makes it special, watching the wings flap,walk, talk etc.  The only soft part on this , is it’s tail and Maine, But this toy is not a cuddle toy, although very cute. It walks better on the kitchen, and dinning room floor, then on the thick carpet,  Includes a short life battery for just trying..It takes  2 AAA and 4 AA batteries  I recommend this Vtach unicorn. Your child will love it, and this will keep them excited, & busy.

My 4 year old granddaughter loves this toy cash register. It allows for a lot of imaginative play and teaches counting. She was able to figure out how to scan stuff and use everything right away. When you scan an item it tells you how many coins to pay, for example it will say "1 coin" and you put a coin in the slot and it will tell you how many you put in. The only slight drawback is that the money amounts aren't real life, so the age on this is a bit limited since around 6 years old most kids are good with counting actual money and coins. So I would say this is great for kids 5 and under and for older kids a toy cash register like the one they sell at ikea would be great for pretend play.

I am skeptical that this is marketed as a toy for children. There is nothing about this that is something I would want a child to play with.  I bought it because I do hot wax art and I have lots of bits of leftover wax that I thought might be fun to mold into a useable block. Dumb idea. This product is somewhat complicated to get together. No child I know could use it without complete parental supervision and help.  I followed the directions and used a couple of the crayons that were included and a scrap of another Crayola crayon on my work table. I ran 3 heating cycles and the wax still wasn't completely melted. The crayon that I added melted at a different rate than the ones included. After about an hour I was left with a semi melted, muddy mess that when poured into the mold didn't even fill it up. At this point I decided to just forget it because I didn't have more time to mess with it.  Poorly conceived idea.

It seemed like a fun car, the LED lights were a bit too bright. Which is easily fixable with a tape.  But the big fail was that the wheel spokes are made of thin plastic. Jumping car as expected would put stress on the wheels, so it cracked within 20 mins of first use. If the manufacturer made the wheels with metal that would have been ideal, also the wheel rubber could use some shock absorption capability to dampen the impact of landings and reduce stress on spokes.  I ended up returning it.

This Kid K'Nex building set is definitely what you would expect from the K'Nex line of toys. This set comes with 115 pieces and 60 different ideas of creatures to build. From the picture on the box, I was expecting to be able to create several different pals at the same time, but there are only enough eyes for 2 pals at any one time or 3 if you have 2 pals with a single eye. There are some pieces that flex and some that are firm. The instruction sheet has pictures to show you how to connect the various pieces, but it does not show you how to make any of their suggested pals. It does show 60 different finished pals, but not how to make them. The pieces are larger than the standard K'Nex and the recommended age range seems appropriate.

My family absolutely loves Pixar movies. They are favorites for sure! These Interactable figures are pretty cool. By pushing a button on the front of the figure, the figure starts talking. If you have another figure and they're facing one another, the 2 figures will talk back and forth. From my playing with them, it seems like there's a pattern of 3 phrases/lines. Figure one says something, then figure two replies, then the first figure says something back. Then just push one of their buttons to keep them talking. There is some repeating of phrases, but never during the three phrases or lines during that button push. Sometimes the characters reply with a logical comeback and others seem totally random. There is usually one line that will call the other figure by name. The figures can be posed. Mr. Incredible's voice is spot on. He can stand up, but he gets knocked over easily. I am looking forward to the release of more Pixar figures!

Short story:  Half the supplies and paint vs the "boys" kits.  I will not be purchasing any 4M kits in the future.  Full story:  Picked up this Unicorn kit and the matching Dino for $10 each (at a local box store).  His kit came with 10 paint colors and 2 packages of plaster - enough for two rounds of dinos.  Hers contained half of that; 1 plaster package and 5 colors of the same size.  It wasn't a mistake;  it's printed in the instructions that the boys kits intentionally come with more!  It really sucked having to explain, apologize and then make this right for my daughter.  So off to the hardware store, where I spent another $10 on a massive bucket of plaster so she could do it a second time with him. Had I not had the dino kit to compare, I still would have been disappointed.  Maybe given it three stars.  $10 for one 15 minute art project is too expensive in my opinion.

This beautiful AQUAMAN MERA DOLL is a good choice for a doll to be played with (not just displayed on a shelf in an unopened box).  The jumpsuit, made of sparkly “scaly” fabric, is removable, so a creative child can dress Mera in her/his own home-sewn outfits.  The doll has a terrific “water-blaster” weapon that snaps onto her right arm.  Mera’s abundant hair is an unnatural shade of bright red, which is the doll’s only negative.  The doll’s features, based on the movie actress’ features, are absolutely beautiful.  The 12” doll size is very good for action-figure play.  This is an affordable action figure (about $21 on Amazon).  If you’re looking for a Mera doll primarily for display, you might prefer the [[ASIN:B01N4U5TD7 Barbie Mera doll]], which has a sculpted costume, but no weapon.

I am a long time Barbie fan. I loved playing with them when I was young, in the 70's. Then in the 90's I collected them. Then in the 2000's I bought them for my little girl. She's graduating from high school next month, but I still love Barbie! I got these for my 5 year old niece to play with when she visits us. I love the variety of body types in this set. I think that's one of the best changes to happen to Barbie ever! This set has 4 different dolls. There's the traditional Barbie that we all love. Then there's the curvy African American doll, a short, petite doll that looks like she could be Hispanic, and a tall , dark hair doll. The thing I love about them (their differences) is also slightly problematic. They all wear different size clothes/shoes. While, that's pretty normal in the real world, it's more difficult in the Barbie world. Some of the dolls could probably get away with sharing clothes, but curvy doll is out of luck. I hope to see more curvy doll size clothes in the future! And if you are planning to gift this, be aware that they don't come in the typical pink Barbie box. They're mounted on brown cardboard that folds into a box shape. There's no colorful pictures or a plastic window. Other than that, I think this is a wonderful set and has been long needed!

I have been using a large, wooden puzzle board with trays for the pieces. When I saw this, I was intrigued with the ability to tightly close it up without destroying the work in progress. The wooden one will keep the puzzle safe, but you have to find a large place to set it when you're not working on it. So I started a 500 piece puzzle on this board. It's got a fuzzy surface which makes it non-slip. That is good at times, but there are times when I will work on a section of a puzzle and slide it to it's destination. That's difficult to do with this one. Instead of puzzle trays, it has flat fuzzy pieces that you can put the pieces on and move around as needed. With my 500 piece puzzle, I couldn't get all of the pieces spread out flat on the combined boards. I had to have some stacked on top of one another. After I had completed the border, I decided to stop for the evening. I put the puzzle boards on top of the main puzzle area where my work was. Then I closed everything up and made sure the velcro pieces were secure. Then I tilted the whole unit at a 90 degree angle. I heard pieces moving around. My heart sank. The border is always a lot of work! I opened it back up and while the pieces had moved around, it was only the pieces that hadn't been assembled yet. The work area with my completed border was still in tact. I think I will feel better leaving this on a flat surface, such as a table when it's not in use. It does keep the cats from sitting on my work and that's a plus. I always had to cover my work with a towel or something with the wooden tray. This is also weighs less than the wooden one which makes it easier to move. Overall, I am pleased with this puzzle storage piece.

At 1000 pieces, this SPRINGBOK EIFFEL MAGIC JIGSAW PUZZLE (1000 Pieces) is definitely an “expert” puzzle.  However, it goes together very quickly, because it has large blocks of unique colors--if you color-sort your pieces at the beginning, the jigsaw morphs into a series of much smaller jigsaws.  My puzzle, received in March 2019, had ALL of the pieces.  No surprise, because I’ve assembled many, many Springbok jigsaws in my lifetime, and have never found one to be less than perfect.  (The negative reviews suggest that Springbok temporarily had a quality control problem with this puzzle design.  I sympathize with the people who received defective puzzles--even though Springbok will replace a defective puzzle, any guarantee is cold comfort when you finish a jigsaw and discover pieces missing.)  Though I wasn’t crazy about the picture, I ended up loving this jigsaw, and am saving it to put together a second time.  It’s really fun to assemble, because the crazy-shaped pieces let you find fits by overall shapes as well as by design colors and patterns.  There are very few dark or ambiguous pieces that are hard to place in a particular area of the design.  The only thing that I didn’t like about the puzzle is that two pieces frequently slide together side-by-side to make holes for the tabs of other pieces.  This means that when you assemble a small area (e.g., the pink automobile in the middle), you can’t easily pick it up to move it to the correct location.  You have to slide the assembly across your tabletop, or slide a sheet of paper under the assembly to support it as it’s moved.  Note that this is a very large (30” x 24”) jigsaw.  Check to make sure that it will fit on your tabletop.

This LEGO MARVEL AVENGERS ULTIMATE QUINJET 76126 BUILDING KIT (838 Pieces) produces a large model (4”H x 15”L x 11”W) with a lot of interesting play features.  My favorite feature is the fold-out rapid stud shooter (6 studs launched when you turn the wheel!), but the stud-shooting trike that rolls out of the rear cargo hatch is a close second.  I also like the two compartments that open on the top of the quinjet to accommodate minifigures.  The one in front is a regular cockpit, the one behind the cockpit is a passenger compartment.  The model also has adjustable wings and rotors that spin.  A large model is always a satisfying build.  This one has a lot of intriguing, innovative construction techniques.  As always, the step-by-step LEGO instructions are crystal clear; if followed correctly, they will yield a rock-solid model for play purposes.  The building instructions for this set can be downloaded into the LEGO Life app.  If you do that, you can build the model using your tablet instead of the printed booklet.

This LEGO DISNEY PRINCESS ELSA’S MAGICAL ICE PALACE 43172 TOY CASTLE BUILDING SET (New 2019; 701 Pieces) is a reissue of the previously discontinued 41148 set of the same name that was originally released in 2017 (also at a retail price of $79.99).  Some of the ad pages in the 43172 building instructions are different, but the models are the same.  The Elsa’s Magical Castle model isn’t large, but it has loads of sparkle and detail; and tons of play potential in the many accessories (e,g., castle furniture, Olaf’s train) and moving parts (e,g., swirling staircase, snow slide). It’s one of my all-time favorite LEGO buildings--one that I have on permanent display (alongside my big Disney castle model).  What I love most about Elsa’s castle is that it incorporates a lot of transparent “Frozen blue” bricks, many of which have embedded silver sparkles.  I love shiny stuff, and Elsa’s shimmering cape is something to behold.  I also love trains, so Olaf’s train is another plus.  The little snowmen’s teeter-totter (near the snow fort) is fun.  I don’t really care for the scary snow monster, but it helps in reenacting scenes from the movie.  Some of the model’s sparkle is added with stickers, but there aren’t a lot of stickers, and they’re easy to apply.  There is a moving slide that opens and closes the tall castle front doors.  There is a spiral staircase that swirls open when you turn a knob.  The figures of Elsa and Anna are true to the cartoon figures in the Frozen film.  This is truly a great, classic LEGO set, based on a great, classic Disney movie.

[[VIDEOID:8ec1360aa58e9f75263c785ff4853349]] [[VIDEOID:fe66008969573ae19b364dcc556f64ce]] This K’NEX EDUCATION STEM EXPLORATIONS: ROLLER COASTER BUILDING SET is challenging to build, but it’s a satisfying building set.  It’s motorized, and it performs really well.  It’s fun to watch the chain pull the roller coaster car up to the top of the tower.  And of course it’s fun to watch the car do the curves and loops of the track by gravity when it’s released.  It took me a couple of days, working off and on, to assemble this roller coaster.  The K’NEX pieces are fiddly to join, and the drawings in the instruction booklet are hard to interpret if you’re new to the K’NEX system.  (The “snowflake” connectors are confusing; the rods must be inserted “just so”; and the drawings sometimes change their directional perspective without warning.)  What I really loved about this set, is that the roller coaster car will often go around the loop more than once without any help.  Three continuous loops is fairly common.  Whether the car continues to loop by itself seems to depend on where the “chain-grabber” underneath the car hits the chain—whether it hits an opening in the chain or not.  If you’re aiming for continuous loops, it helps to hold the base down.  The K’NEX rods are thin, so the roller coaster support structure is kind of flimsy.  However, the connections are tight enough that you can pick the whole structure up and move it without destroying it.  A few connections may come loose, but they’re easy to reconnect.  The roller coaster is a large structure (30” wide, 16” deep, 25” high), so you need quite a bit of space to build and play with it.  The three experiments (one included in the box, two downloadable from the K’NEX web site) add to the educational value of this building set.  The first experiment suggests adding different amounts of weight to the car to test how added mass affects the car’s ability to run the loops.  For the second and third experiments, you rebuild the coaster into a long gravity ramp, and then into a half pipe, to learn more about how a car behaves on a straight or U-shaped track.  Neither of these builds makes use of the motor, which is why the roller coaster build is far and away the best.  When you open the box, keep the contents of each bag together, more or less.  You’ll save time if you don’t dump all of the pieces out in a big pile on the tabletop.  The pieces that are the same type are grouped together in the plastic bags—chain links, rods of certain lengths, red connectors, blue connectors, spacers, etc.  When I’m assembling a big project like this one, with a lot of pieces, I use disposable plastic dinner and salad plates to hold the similar, or the same-colored, parts.  A nice thing about this K’NEX set is that each different type of connector is a different color (the orange connectors are different from the yellow connectors, and so on), so it’s enough to check the color of the piece when you’re following the instructions.

This MAINSTREET CLASSICS DUTCHMAN 3-IN-1 COMBO FOLDING BOARD GAME SET delivers pretty good value for the money (about $37 on Amazon). The case folds closed (with a handle) so that it can hold wooden checkers, wooden chess pieces, wooden dice cups, and backgammon dice.  My game set has none of the quality control issues complained of by other reviewers (no blobs of glue, loose hinges, etc.).  The chess pieces won’t win any design awards, but they are playable because they are easily recognizable as pawn, knight, rook, etc.  The checkers are small, but are reasonably sized for a portable game set.  The case is sanded smooth and has a lacquer finish.  I think this is a great game set to carry along on vacation, or to leave year-round at a cabin or second home.  It is a decent starter set for kids, especially for kids interested in learning chess.

This 4D CITYSCAPE CHICAGO SKYLINE PUZZLE is great in concept, but falls way short in the execution.  It's a three-part puzzle.  First you assemble a standard jigsaw (an 1867 Chicago street map).  Next you assemble a foam-based jigsaw with identical interlocking tabs (a modern Chicago street map) that goes atop the first jigsaw.  Third you add three-dimensional plastic buildings to the foam jigsaw.  The first jigsaw is difficult, especially in the large blue Lake Michigan area, but the street names and the boats on the lake help enough to make this an expert-but-fairly-easy jigsaw.  The second foam jigsaw comes partly assembled, with several large pieces, and is very easy, taking less than an hour. (Unless you separate all the smallest foam street-grid pieces, which will require a lot of close comparison with the color map, and a lot of extra unsatisfying puzzle time, to reassemble.)  The third part, adding the buildings, is where the whole puzzle concept breaks down.  Unless you're familiar with Chicago already, the buildings are hard to identify by comparing the plastic pieces with the photographs on the color buildings poster.  You have to identify each building by finding its numbered location on the color map, and then matching the appearance of the roof to the line drawing of the roof found in the punch-out foam puzzle piece at the location in the foam map. (With some similar buildings, this is quite difficult, and you have to check the size of the plastic bases as well.  It also helps to know how to read architectural diagrams.)  Finally, inserting the buildings is a major problem.  If you punch out the foam piece where the building goes in, you will NOT be able to tell how the building is oriented to the street and the buildings around it.  If you don't punch out the foam piece, but merely jam the building down onto it, the building will fall over whenever you jostle the foam as you add more buildings.  Another problem is that some historic buildings are gold and the rest are silver-gray.  The puzzle kit also includes a set of silver-gray "generic" buildings that you can add to make the Chicago downtown look more like a skyscraper city.  However, you don't know when these buildings were built, so inserting them destroys the historical timeline that helps you see how Chicago grew.  I was really excited about this puzzle, because I wanted to see all the buildings added after I departed Chicago in the sixties.  However, adding the buildings was fiddly and far too much of a hassle, so I bailed on the project after 25 buildings.  (If I ever decide to go back and finish it, I will glue the buildings in place atop the foam.)  In sum, although the standard jigsaw is fun to assemble, it is overpriced at the cost of the entire kit (about $30 on Amazon).  The rest of the kit was a big disappointment. Now I think I'll try to find a book of photographs that will show me how Chicago has grown since 1867, or since the sixties.

My 17 year old daughter is currently obsessed with Jurassic World/Park and dinosaurs. I thought this would be a lot of fun for her. She was able to assemble the dino by herself in about thirty minutes.  The pieces are a sturdy flexible plastic. You have to download an app and then the step by step instructions are provided. I personally thought that was an ingenious way to do that. Once it was assembled, she was able to control the dino through the app. You can do things like change the color of lights in the eyes, move forward and backward, and more. You write little programs to get it to follow a chain of commands. There are challenges you can do that unlock various behaviors. We loved watching the cats' reactions to it. (They were not fans!). It comes with a USB cable for charging. There's a skin to go over the USB plug that looks like meat. The port to charge the dino is in the mouth, so it looks like you're feeding it meat! Sounds made by the dino go through the app. My daughter has been thoroughly enjoying her raptor. A younger child may need some guidance and assistance, but it's definitely within their ability. Overall, I would recommend this for any dinosaur fan who is interested in robotics or technology.

The package"arrived this evening, I'd snuck into the bathroom and positioned it perfectly in drape of the seat. Unfortunately, boyfriend did not use the bathroom immediately despite my estimations, in fact HOURS went by and suddenly I heard a flushing.  I asked if he flushed it. He looked at me. I now know this man loves me above all things as there was no judgement in his expression, just a solemn nod. Despite my laughter, he was still slightly shell shocked and not sure how to respond. He wasn't sure to believe that it was plastic. "It looked so real," he lamented with regret because now he wishes he could have kept the poo.  So far, no plumbing issues resulted in the flushing. My daughter colors a lot, and we have been testing the various new Bic markers a lot! These are our least favorite.  First, they really aren’t that much bigger of an easy grip. The barrel is the same size as the Child’s First ultra washable Bics and the Brush tip Bics, it’s only slightly larger than the Ultra Washable Medium markers. Also the box for these is really flimsy and was already coming open at the bottom when we received them. Most importantly, the tip is not great for large areas of coloring (like classic Crayola markers) or for details. My 5 year old was really impressed with the Medium markers and loved the detailing she was able to do with the fine tip on those. She also loves the brush tip line. Those have been great for both drawings and for practicing writing and numbers. If you are looking for good first markers, I’d stick with Crayola. If you want something for more detail, give the Medium or Brush Tip Bics a try.

[[VIDEOID:moMO0O00W4S41F]]This Springbok "Hatley Park" jigsaw puzzle was much more difficult than I expected, even for a 1000-piece puzzle.  The large areas of red tree leaves (at the top) and fallen leaves along the pathway (at the bottom) each have a lot of similarly colored pieces that take patience to sort.  Also, the individual puzzle pieces (no two the same) are cut so that the pieces don't form regular rows and columns across the entire puzzle (unlike the cut of Ravensburger puzzles).  As you put "Hatley Park" together, you must hunt for specific pieces that have quite unusual and irregular sizes and shapes.  Often you must put two pieces together to be able to see the shape of the third piece, which interlocks with both.  Even the straight-edged border of this puzzle is something of a challenge!  As you can see from the video slide show that accompanies this review, some of the smaller border pieces don't look like border pieces at all.  If you enjoy working difficult jigsaw puzzles, and like the puzzle photo (as I do), then you ought to have fun assembling "Hatley Park" even though it takes some extra effort.  Like all Springbok puzzles, "Hatley Park" is a high-quality, carefully-manufactured jigsaw puzzle that has a well-thought-out overall design.

This LITTLEBITS BUBBLE BOT STARTER KIT (9 Piece) has everything you need to make a cute electric bubble-blower, but why is it so expensive?  (The kit is about $40 on Amazon.)  And at that price, why aren’t the simple instructions included in a printed leaflet inside the box?  And at that price, why isn’t the solid purple tube printed with the dragon/tiger/shark design shown on the packaging and in the advertising?  I was able to produce basketball-sized bubbles, using dish detergent mixed with a small amount of water.  The instructions don’t include a recipe for the bubble solution, so you have to experiment to get a soap bubble mixture that works.  The bot works best if you put the solution into a bowl (so you can pull the tube out freely).  To blow big bubbles, you need to speed up the fan gradually, using the dimmer slider.  Assembling the bot is a snap.  You line up the circuit parts according to the photo in the instructions, and they click together because they’re magnetized.  If they’re lined up wrong, the magnetic poles will force them apart, so you know you must reverse one of the circuit parts.  An 8-year-old child can surely assemble this kit.  However, I don’t see how this kit teaches much of anything beyond clicking magnets together and flipping switches.  The instructions don’t say anything about the physics principles involved.  Also, it was a big disappointment to find that the tube needs to be decorated by the kit buyer.  That might be okay if a stencil or template for the design shown in the ads were included, but there’s nothing beyond the picture on the package.  The printable assembly instructions that you get from the littleBits web site include a link to another bubble bot that you can build from LEGO bricks and some of the parts from this kit.  However, the LEGO bot requires a littleBits light sensor part, and this doesn’t appear to be available for purchase except as part of an expensive set of littleBits components.  Too bad, as that bubble bot is more interesting than this one.  UPDATE 08-06-18:  The pdf version of the assembly instructions (if you can find them--try Googling "littleBits bubble bot instructions") has more STEM teaching information than the web site instructions that I used.  The pdf explains why the circuit components need to be assembled in a certain order, and shows photos of real-life devices that use the same types of components as the components in the kit.  Why these instructions are NOT included in the kit is a mystery to me.

EDIT (7/3/21):  Star rating downgraded from 3 stars to 2 stars.  The photo provided in the box does NOT show sufficient detail in the very dark and blurry areas.  Assembly requires constant squinting at individual pieces to find minuscule identifying features. This jigsaw took me 2 weeks to assemble, instead of my usual 5-6 days for a seriously challenging jigsaw.  This PUZZQUEST JIGSAW PUZZLE (1000 PIeces, “Stroll”) is one of the most difficult jigsaw puzzles that I’ve ever encountered.  It is difficult because (1) it has many similarly-colored pieces that are almost identically shaped; (2) the correct pieces do NOT click together solidly (unlike Ravensburger or other high-quality jigsaw puzzle pieces); and (3) at least half of the puzzle consists of out-of-focus greenery in dark, muddled colors.  In assembling the “easy” border and left side (in clear focus) of the puzzle, I must have made 15-20 incorrect joins that needed to be corrected later.  Assembling by shapes is hampered by the similarity of neighboring pieces--absent a solid click-together, you can easily join two pieces incorrectly and build on them for a while before your error becomes apparent.  The colors of the pieces will NOT alert you to the incorrect joins.  The puzzle is a very awkward size.  It is very tall, so you have to stand up and lean over it to work on the top quarter.  Be sure to measure your work area to make sure you have enough space to accommodate the height.  The only reason to buy this puzzle is to prove that you’re up to any jigsaw puzzle challenge.  To accommodate 1000 pieces, the photo is blown up so much that even the most distinct areas have several pieces.  Thus the assembly is slow-going, and not particularly enjoyable.  Pre-sorting the pieces doesn’t help to speed things up, because half the puzzle consists of out-of-focus greenery in muddled dark colors.

I work in a Special Needs classroom with a couple of autistic boys who love to color with colored pencils. They tend to use certain colors more often and then because of the firm pressure that they use when coloring, we are constantly out of those certain colors. This week, one of the boys nearly had a meltdown because we didn't have the right shade of blue. This set of 8 colors allows us to restock the blues, reds, black, browns, and yellows that we go through the most without adding more of the colors we aren't using as quickly. I would buy a box of single colors, such as all blue, if they were packaged that way. As far as the pencils go, Crayola is our go-to brand in the classroom. They sharpen easily & don't have as much breakage as some brands. The colors are vibrant.

The quality of this game is great. The cardboard pieces are thick, the box is sturdy, the buttons are nice. But it’s just too hard and frustrating. Three of the nine options on the spinner are negative - either give a button to the player on your right, put one button back, or put ALL buttons back. This makes the game way too frustrating. I started playing this with my 4 and 5 year old and we quickly each had 2-3 of the 5 needed buttons, but for the next 15 minutes we kept spinning negative results enough to keep emptying our Pete boards and staying somewhere between 0-3 buttons. My 4 year was finally lucky enough to finish the game and I begrudgingly tried one more round to see if the first was a fluke. It went very similarly with my kids being frustrated that they often would get up to 2-3 buttons then have to put them all back, over and over for a long 15 minutes. We also have The Sneaky Snacky Squirrel by this company. It’s very similar in working fine motor skills and a great quality, and it is not frustrating at all!! Of the 10 spinner options for that game, only 2 are negative causing you to put acorns back. And my kids actually enjoy it. That one is SO MUCH BETTER!!! I would definitely avoid this Pete the Cat one!

You press a button on one of the ear cups, and it starts playing a somewhat noisy and annoying background sound. There is a timer feature built in, so the noise stops after a minute has passed which signals that your turn is over. The headphones don't feel very sturdy, but they are okay. There are 600 cards which have four phrases on each card. We found it difficult to hear the whispering when wearing the headphones, which is desired. If you correctly guess the right phrase, you get to keep the card which is later counted as points. Team with the most points at the end wins. There's instructions for playing with 4 to 8 people as well as for just two. It was funny what some of us thought was being whispered! My daughter is looking forward to playing this at her next game night with friends.

This is a horrible toy. My two year old daughter got it as a birthday gift from her godfather, who bought it because of the fantastic reviews. It arrived broken -- one of the paw pad sensors doesn't work unless you squeeze it just right, with two fingers. In the past Fisher Price has given me fantastic service, so I'm sure they'll take care of that problem when I get around to calling.  The really terrible thing about this puppy is that when you hit a sensor, the toy stops whatever it was doing and starts a new thing. The sensors are super sensitive, and will go off if you just brush up against the toy. My daughter wants to hear the songs all the way through, and sing along, but of course she wants to be able to hold or touch the puppy at the same time. So the song stops, then it takes like five tries to cycle around to the song she wants, then she hugs the puppy and the song stops again. After about ten minutes of this she was in tears. I've never see my little girl so distraught -- she's not a kid who gets discouraged or cries much, but she just collapsed into my arms in tears of frustration. I felt awful for her.  Other things I don't like about it: 1. The "this little piggy" chant is turned into "this little puppy," so now my daughter has started saying that, and I prefer the classic rhyme. 2. There's a whole part that says "blue ear," "yellow foot," "green hand," when you hit those body parts. But because it cuts off when you hit the sensors, it can sound like "blue -- foot," when the foot is actually yellow. My daughter repeated "blue foot," and I had to correct her. 3. It says "I love you". I generally don't like inanimate objects saying they love my child.  If Fisher Price just made the slight change of having this puppy complete what he's doing before starting the next activity, we'd probably love it!

I was super excited to receive this item because it claims to light up and turn in multiple ways, however assembly was a pain!  Assembly of this product was so painstaking, I ended up having a couple of dented finger nails, some splinters, and a bunch of broken and scratched up skin.  This item requires a lot of finger strength, yet it requires gentle assembly.  There are certain parts during assembly where it requires me to gently slide in one part, yet push it in with force.  the longitudinal wood pieces came in 12 pieces, but to assemble, it must fit into 5 different slots.  I broke about 9 little slots in 9 difference pieces.  The instructions were all pictures, but certain parts of the instruction must be studied in detail!!!  I studied certain steps and was confident; turned out, I was wrong and had to break it apart which caused more broken wooden pieces.  The last part, one of the pieces didn't come fully cut, so when I pushed a piece in, it snapped.  Overall, if this item I assembled was not examined in detail, nobody would see any of the cracks.  I like what I assembled and it did turn out great, but I refuse to ever do this again!  and it wont turn!  I waxed the pieces that required waxing, but as hard as I try to turn the knobs, the globe wont turn!  If I force, it'll turn a little bit, but I feel like it might break!

Where to start.  They successfully removed all the tape from the pink cardboard package which contained a toy that is surprisingly from an Australian Company, Moose Toys, which was made in China.  They counted the small globes and were surprised to see only 4, since there are 5 carriers for globes on the wheel.  The kids were very disappointed that they would not be making a full ferris wheel.  They opened up the instruction booklet and could not figure out the words.  With a little help from me via Google Translate, we discovered that the booklet was written in Romanian which we do not speak.  They did not want to type in everything for translation or search for a person who speaks Romanian to interpret the manual.  Needless to say, they hate this and they do not dislike much.  Below is what I typed to decipher the instructions for the [[ASIN:B00IR7NFUU Glitzi Globes Mega Display Ferris Wheel]]:  Romanian asamblati va propria roata fermecata cu sclipici introduceti cu fermitate pilonii rotii fermecate cu sclipici /  assemble your own glitter wheel magic wheel Firmly enchanted glitter pillars  The little globes are much, much smaller than they appear and the little characters are unremarkable and unmemorable.

My sister was telling me about a similar kit that she was working on. Then I found out that my best friend was working on a kit like this. I had to get in on the fun! I am absolutely fascinated by them. The kit includes all the pieces to make the items in the garden coffee shop. The items are not pre-made so you're starting at the beginning. All the pieces are provided as well as good, detailed instructions. The instructions show you step by step how to make the pieces. The wording is a bit choppy at time, but I am able to get the gist of it. The pictures are helpful! Some tools are included, but I am using my own craft detail scissors that seem better than the ones provided. Glue is not provided either. My sister recommend craft glue which is what I'm using and it seems to be adequate. Patience is definitely required! It's fun watching this model set come to shape!
Son loved this upon receiving. Had his keys on the keychain one day when he dropped them and the head broke off the figure. Poor construction for a keychain. Who doesn't drop their keys?

I recently started working puzzles regularly. I had never really been a fan before, but with the pandemic, it was something different to do. This puzzle is a smaller one with only 500 pieces. The size of the pieces is a little on the small side, but it's not too bad. It does come with a large fold out poster which is a necessity for me when I'm puzzling. It's much easier to hold and look at a poster than have to hold onto the box lid. The pieces are marked on the back to speed up separating which was nice. That saved a lot of time. Overall, it's a decent puzzle.

I recently sat down to read with my 6 year old niece. She struggled with a lot of common sight words. As a former elementary school teacher, I wanted to help her. These are excellent flash cards! There are 6 boxes of cards, one for each level PreK through 3rd grade. Then the 6th box is full of blank ones that are reusable (dry erase marker). All of the cards are sturdy and easy to read. Each card is double sided to accommodate more words!  I used to make flash cards like these for my kids when they were younger. They're both in college now, and don't need my help with flash cards. The cards come with 6 rings so you can remove and rearrange the cards as needed. That makes it easy to keep the ones that your child is still struggling with together for quick access. I highly recommend these!

For children who are good with imaginative play, this can be a fun toy.

Returning again. But this time to manufacturer.  Adorable doll. Super cute voice, movements, interactions. 9yo received as gift from Santa (has been asking for a baby alive for no less than four years). She opened and played with it once the day after Christmas, then “put it to bed fresh and clean”. The next day, it did not work. We had figured it was because she did use it for two hours the first day- guessed that the store batteries died. We replaced the batteries with brand new Duracell‘s… Nothing. It still did not work. Through her tears from disappointment, and a quick explanation that Santa needs help after Xmas (because why would Santa send a toy that would break!?) We utilized Kohls return to Amazon. We received the replacement doll yesterday. Same exact doll. Again she played with it for two hours- under the close supervision of myself to ensure she was following the directions. She put it to bed fresh and clean, and at 8 o’clock this morning she came into my room crying that it’s not working. We tried new batteries, switch placements, pressing and holding the belly. Nothing. I called Hasbro this morning and they are sending a return label, and a different replacement baby alive doll. Overall I’m extremely disappointed in this product. I was unable to find anyone else having the same issue. I highly doubt it is result of my daughter playing. This is designed for children ages three and up. She is a nine-year-old who is extremely cautious. This was her only Santa gift and it has caused lots of heartache. We do our best to downplay the frustration. We understand malfunctions happen. I just don’t want anybody else to have the same issue and feel alone like we did. I would not recommend this doll specifically. Fingers crossed the third replacement (A different design) works more than once.  Update: we purchased a 3rd replacement happy hungry baby from a local store. A different hair color than our first two. Hoping it’s a different production line. It’s still working on day two! My daughter squealed this morning with excitement “she works! She works!”  Shes used the 3rd happy hungry baby (brunette) for weeks now- almost daily. Its been working wonderfully! Still unbelievable that the first two were defective (Black hair). Raised to 2 stars for the fact she loves it. The headache and hassle of 2 dolls would make it where i would -cautiously- recommend. Keep the box (and receipt) for at least a month.

My daughter is in college studying wildlife biology. She made a comment to me over the holiday break that she wanted to learn to identify animal tracks. When I saw this, I thought it would be perfect! While it is geared toward kids, there's nothing childish about it. It has some information about the animal tracks to help you distinguish between different animals. The tracks are not to size, but with larger animals, that would be not realistic. You'd have cards of all sizes! The quality of the cards is good. They're not flimsy by any means. There are 3 sets of cards. It  seems to be divided up into farm animals, woodland animals, and plains animals, though I may be incorrect on the actual groups, that's just my guess. This would be a great educational activity for kids of all ages!
